author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: This is a great looking copper coating, and a much better application than sheet copper or brass.  I sort-of wish you'd done the entire frame that way. I think that the orchid food is acidic, and I'm sure there's probably a simpler chemical way to oxidize the surface.

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: You could apply several coats, then buff it. You would have to use a very fine abrasive paste though.

replies.author: @luakhurst208
replies.text: @VinoVeritas_ Not sure that would work as the copper only adheres to the available iron in the steel. Once that's covered with the copper, you can't get it any thicker.

author: @LOLLOF90
text: Would that work with alluminium?

replies.author: @luakhurst208
replies.text: No.  The copper binds to the iron in the steel.  This means that once the iron particles are coated, you can't deposit any more copper on top.  It's a mini battery all by itself with the formate salts and the iron.  Aluminium (Brit here, sorry about the spelling for you) isn't magnetic so it wouldn't work.  Look at Peen plating for aluminium.

replies.author: @FolieduNord
replies.text:
Same here: I can't find it either... There's a few on making some kind of ink and and making copper nanoparticles, but nothing that ends up producing the mysterious "coppering gel". In different video's where it's used it also seems to vary in colour from bright blue to light green..?!
Why is nobody willing to simply give us a usefull link instead of insisting it easy to find...?

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: @FolieduNord There isn't a specific video or set of videos showing you a step by step method of making the coppering gel. That's why I suggested to search for all videos by Robert Murray Smith containing the word 'copper'. The colour of the gel will depend on the type of copper salt used.

I've by now seen some of the different videos and I still don't find it clear what kind of mixture he is making when and for what purpose...
